original_registry_entry.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
original_registry_entry.exact_document_title: Pressure ulcers: prevention and management — Recommendations
original_registry_entry.version: NICE clinical guideline CG179; last reviewed 30 November 2018
original_registry_entry.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE adaptation
original_registry_entry.population: Adults, children, young people, infants, and neonates at risk of or with a pressure ulcer.
original_registry_entry.clinical_setting: Primary, community, emergency, secondary, and tertiary care pressure-risk and wound assessment.
original_registry_entry.applicability_note: Exact for documenting pressure-ulcer risk factors, skin assessment, ulcer surface area, depth, undermining, category, photography, and repeated assessment. It does not generate a risk score, diagnosis, treatment, dressing, offloading, referral, or urgency.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.7 — adult risk and skin assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting mobility, previous or current pressure ulcer, nutritional status, cognition, sensory impairment, posture, deformity, skin integrity, colour change, heat, firmness, moisture, and pain only when assessed.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1–1.4.3 and 1.5.1–1.5.3 — ulcer measurement and categorisation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting pressure-ulcer surface area, validated measurement or photography, estimated depth, undermining, clinician-assigned category, and repeat assessment without automatically calculating or assigning a category.

title: Pressure ulcers: prevention and management — Recommendations
publisher: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE adaptation
population: Adults, children, young people, infants, and neonates at risk of or with a pressure ulcer.
intended_setting: Primary, community, emergency, secondary, and tertiary care pressure-risk and wound assessment.
